ui.noCompare: No comparable events in this window.
ui.seedNote: Seed data is for MVP iteration; dates/locations are approximate.

rulerBio.qinshihuang: First emperor of a unified Qin; standardized systems and central authority.
rulerBio.qiner: Second Qin ruler; succession crisis and rebellions accelerated Qin's collapse.
rulerBio.hangaozu: Founder of the Han dynasty; rebuilt governance after the Qin–Chu wars.
rulerBio.hanwudi: Expansion, state monopolies, and Confucian statecraft shaped the Han imperial model.
rulerBio.suiwen: Unified China after centuries of division; laid administrative groundwork for the Tang.
rulerBio.suiyang: Known for Grand Canal projects and costly campaigns; reign ended amid widespread rebellion.
rulerBio.tangtaizong: Early Tang ruler associated with stable governance and expansion.
rulerBio.wuzetian: The only woman to rule China as emperor in her own right; significant court and state reforms.
rulerBio.tangxuanzong: A long reign with cultural flourishing but later destabilized by major rebellion.
rulerBio.songtaizu: Founder of the Song; consolidated power and reduced warlord fragmentation.
rulerBio.songhuizong: Patron of arts; his reign ended with the Jingkang crisis and northern collapse.
rulerBio.yuanshizu: Established the Yuan dynasty as a Chinese imperial state; reorganized governance over a vast realm.
rulerBio.mingtaizu: Founder of the Ming; rebuilt institutions and centralized authority.
rulerBio.mingchengzu: Moved the capital to Beijing; sponsored major maritime expeditions.
rulerBio.kangxi: Oversaw consolidation and long-term stability; patron of learning and statecraft.
rulerBio.qianlong: Peak-era Qing ruler; major cultural projects alongside rising fiscal and social strains.
rulerBio.zhou-wu: Overthrew Shang and founded the Zhou dynasty; associated with the conquest at Muye.
rulerBio.zhou-cheng: Succeeded early; the Duke of Zhou served as regent and stabilized the new regime.
rulerBio.zhou-kang: Traditionally linked to a period of consolidation and relative stability after founding.
rulerBio.zhou-zhao: Known in tradition for southern campaigns; his death is linked to a failed expedition.
rulerBio.zhou-mu: A long reign in later tradition; associated with westward expeditions and royal authority.
rulerBio.zhou-gong: Continued Western Zhou governance; reign sits within the mid-Zhou sequence of kings.
rulerBio.zhou-yih: A Western Zhou king; later sources describe pressures on royal authority.
rulerBio.zhou-xiao: A short reign; part of the later Western Zhou succession.
rulerBio.zhou-yi: Later Western Zhou king; period often described as weakening royal power.
rulerBio.zhou-li: His policies led to unrest; deposition triggered the Gonghe Regency, a key chronological marker.
rulerBio.zhou-gonghe: A regency period after King Li’s exile; often treated as a key anchor year (841 BCE).
rulerBio.zhou-xuan: Traditionally associated with partial restoration of royal authority after the regency.
rulerBio.zhou-you: The last Western Zhou king; his death in the Quanrong attack led to the Eastern Zhou transition.
rulerBio.zhou-ping: Moved the Zhou court east to Luoyi (Luoyang) after turmoil, beginning the Eastern Zhou era.
rulerBio.zhou-huan: An early Eastern Zhou king during the continued decline of royal authority and rising regional states.
rulerBio.zhou-zhuang: Reign saw intensifying interstate competition and weakening Zhou central control.
rulerBio.zhou-xi: A transitional mid–Spring and Autumn king as regional politics became dominant.
rulerBio.zhou-hui: A Spring and Autumn king amid complex court–state relations and emerging hegemon politics.
rulerBio.zhou-xiang: Long reign in which the hegemon system operated; Zhou increasingly relied on powerful states.
rulerBio.zhou-qing: A king in the mid-late Spring and Autumn period amid continued weakening of the court.
rulerBio.zhou-kuang: Short reign during ongoing dominance of regional states.
rulerBio.zhou-ding: Late Spring and Autumn king as alliances and rivalry intensified among major states.
rulerBio.zhou-jian: A late-period king as Zhou influence continued to decline.
rulerBio.zhou-ling: Late Spring and Autumn king amid internal court issues and interstate tensions.
rulerBio.zhou-jing: Late-period king as the balance among great states evolved rapidly.
rulerBio.zhou-dao: Reigned at the end of the Spring and Autumn period; often used as a reference point for the transition toward the Warring States era.

rulerHighlight.qinshihuang: Unification, standardization (weights, measures, writing), and commandery-county administration.
rulerHighlight.qiner: Court intrigue and uprisings; Qin rule ends shortly after.
rulerHighlight.hangaozu: Consolidation after Chu–Han contention; early Han institutions take shape.
rulerHighlight.hanwudi: Territorial expansion and institutional reforms; long, influential reign.
rulerHighlight.suiwen: Reunification and institutional setup; set the stage for Tang governance.
rulerHighlight.suiyang: Grand Canal expansion; overreach in labor and war contributed to collapse.
rulerHighlight.tangtaizong: Governance reforms and a strong early Tang state.
rulerHighlight.wuzetian: Reforms and patronage; reshaped court politics and examinations.
rulerHighlight.tangxuanzong: Cultural prosperity early; later period linked to An Lushan Rebellion.
rulerHighlight.songtaizu: Centralization and integration of military power under civil control.
rulerHighlight.songhuizong: Cultural achievements; political-military weakness exposed by Jin invasion.
rulerHighlight.yuanshizu: Formal founding of Yuan and administrative integration.
rulerHighlight.mingchengzu: Beijing capital era; Zheng He voyages.
rulerHighlight.kangxi: Consolidation, frontier governance, and long stability.
rulerHighlight.qianlong: Cultural projects and territorial management at Qing height.
rulerHighlight.zhou-wu: Conquest of Shang; initiation of Zhou political order.
rulerHighlight.zhou-cheng: Regency governance; suppression of early rebellion and consolidation.
rulerHighlight.zhou-kang: Consolidation phase in early Zhou tradition.
rulerHighlight.zhou-zhao: Southern campaigning tradition; limits of royal expansion.
rulerHighlight.zhou-mu: Long reign in tradition; royal authority and westward lore.
rulerHighlight.zhou-gong: Mid-Zhou continuity of rites and governance.
rulerHighlight.zhou-yi: Royal weakening often noted in later historiography.
rulerHighlight.zhou-li: Unrest leading to exile; Gonghe Regency begins.
rulerHighlight.zhou-gonghe: Chronological anchor around 841 BCE; transitional governance.
rulerHighlight.zhou-xuan: Partial restoration narrative; continued frontier conflicts.
rulerHighlight.zhou-ping: Eastern relocation to Luoyi; start of Eastern Zhou.
rulerHighlight.zhou-huan: Continued decline of Zhou royal authority.
rulerHighlight.zhou-hui: Alliances and hegemon politics become more regular.
rulerHighlight.zhou-xiang: Long reign during operation of the hegemon system.
rulerHighlight.zhou-ding: Frequent alliances and rivalry in late period.
rulerHighlight.zhou-jing: Great-state balance evolves in late period.
rulerHighlight.zhou-dao: A reference point in the transition toward Warring States.

sa-770-zhou-east.title: Zhou court moves east to Luoyi (Luoyang)
sa-770-zhou-east.summary: The royal court relocates east after turmoil, marking the conventional start of the Spring and Autumn period.

sa-656-qihuan.title: Duke Huan of Qi becomes hegemon
sa-656-qihuan.summary: Qi rises as a leading state, associated with reforms and the hegemon system.

sa-632-chengpu.title: Battle of Chengpu
sa-632-chengpu.summary: Jin defeats Chu in a pivotal battle, shaping the balance among competing states.

sa-551-confucius.title: Birth of Confucius (trad.)
sa-551-confucius.summary: Traditionally dated to this year; later central to East Asian ethical and political thought.

sa-506-boju.title: Battle of Boju
sa-506-boju.summary: Wu defeats Chu and briefly captures its capital, highlighting volatile interstate rivalry.

ws-453-three-jin.title: Partition of Jin (Three Jin)
ws-453-three-jin.summary: Power consolidates into Han, Zhao, and Wei—accelerating the Warring States order.

ws-356-shangyang-reform.title: Shang Yang reforms in Qin
ws-356-shangyang-reform.summary: Legalist reforms strengthen Qin institutions, military organization, and agriculture.

ws-341-maling.title: Battle of Maling
ws-341-maling.summary: Qi defeats Wei with ambush tactics; remembered for Sun Bin's strategy.

ws-260-changping.title: Battle of Changping
ws-260-changping.summary: Qin's victory over Zhao signals dominance and foreshadows imperial unification.

ws-221-unification.title: Qin unifies the warring states
ws-221-unification.summary: Qin conquers rival states; Ying Zheng proclaims himself First Emperor.

qin-214-lingqu.title: Lingqu Canal constructed (trad.)
qin-214-lingqu.summary: A major canal project supports logistics and consolidation in the south.

qin-210-first-emperor-death.title: Death of Qin Shi Huang
qin-210-first-emperor-death.summary: Succession crises and revolts soon follow, undermining Qin rule.

qin-209-dazexiang.title: Dazexiang Uprising
qin-209-dazexiang.summary: A rebellion led by Chen Sheng and Wu Guang becomes a symbol of anti-Qin unrest.

han-202-han-founded.title: Liu Bang founds the Han dynasty
han-202-han-founded.summary: After the Chu–Han contention, Liu Bang becomes Emperor Gaozu and establishes Han.

han-154-seven-states.title: Rebellion of the Seven States
han-154-seven-states.summary: A major revolt by regional kings is suppressed, strengthening central authority.

han-138-zhangqian.title: Zhang Qian sent to the Western Regions
han-138-zhangqian.summary: Diplomatic missions open routes and knowledge that later underpin Silk Road connections.

eh-105-paper.title: Cai Lun improves papermaking (trad.)
eh-105-paper.summary: Papermaking improvements support administration and cultural diffusion.

eh-184-yellow-turbans.title: Yellow Turban Rebellion
eh-184-yellow-turbans.summary: A massive uprising weakens central control and accelerates fragmentation.

eh-220-han-ends.title: End of Eastern Han
eh-220-han-ends.summary: Emperor Xian abdicates; Cao Pi founds Wei, beginning the Three Kingdoms era.

sui-589-unify.title: Sui reunifies China
sui-589-unify.summary: The Sui defeats the Chen and ends the Southern and Northern Dynasties division.

sui-605-grand-canal.title: Grand Canal construction expands
sui-605-grand-canal.summary: Large-scale canal works link north and south, boosting transport and taxation capacity.

tang-618-founded.title: Founding of the Tang dynasty
tang-618-founded.summary: Li Yuan establishes the Tang, beginning a major era of imperial rebuilding.

tang-755-anlushan.title: An Lushan Rebellion
tang-755-anlushan.summary: A devastating rebellion that weakens Tang central power and reshapes society and military governance.

song-960-founded.title: Founding of the Song dynasty
song-960-founded.summary: Zhao Kuangyin becomes Emperor Taizu and unifies much of China after the Five Dynasties period.

song-1127-jingkang.title: Jingkang Incident
song-1127-jingkang.summary: The Jin captures Kaifeng; Northern Song collapses and the court retreats south.

yuan-1271-founded.title: Yuan dynasty proclaimed
yuan-1271-founded.summary: Kublai Khan proclaims the Yuan dynasty, integrating Mongol rule into a Chinese dynastic framework.

ming-1368-founded.title: Founding of the Ming dynasty
ming-1368-founded.summary: Zhu Yuanzhang (Hongwu) establishes the Ming and ends Yuan rule in China proper.

ming-1405-zhenghe.title: Zheng He voyages begin
ming-1405-zhenghe.summary: State-sponsored maritime expeditions project Ming influence across the Indian Ocean.

qing-1644-founded.title: Qing takes Beijing
qing-1644-founded.summary: The Qing enters Beijing, beginning consolidation over China after the Ming collapse.

qing-1840-opium.title: First Opium War
qing-1840-opium.summary: War with Britain marks a major turning point, intensifying foreign pressure and internal challenges.

roc-1911-xinhai.title: Xinhai Revolution
roc-1911-xinhai.summary: Revolutionary uprisings end the Qing dynasty and launch the Republic era.

prc-1949-founded.title: Founding of the People's Republic of China
prc-1949-founded.summary: The PRC is proclaimed in Beijing, beginning a new political era.

prc-1978-reform.title: Reform and Opening-up begins
prc-1978-reform.summary: Economic reforms and opening policies start, transforming society and development trajectories.

wz-1046-muye.title: Battle of Muye
wz-1046-muye.summary: Zhou forces defeat Shang, opening the era of Western Zhou rule.

wz-1042-regency.title: Regency of the Duke of Zhou (trad.)
wz-1042-regency.summary: Early regency politics stabilize the new dynasty after succession.

wz-1040-fengjian.title: Enfeoffment & feudal order expands
wz-1040-fengjian.summary: The Zhou enfeoffment system and ritual-political order are consolidated across regions.

wz-841-gonghe.title: Gonghe Regency begins (841 BCE)
wz-841-gonghe.summary: After King Li’s exile, a regency begins; 841 BCE is a key anchor year in Chinese chronology.

wz-771-quanrong.title: Quanrong sack Haojing (771 BCE)
wz-771-quanrong.summary: The capital is attacked and King You dies; Western Zhou collapses, paving the way for Eastern Zhou.

sa-707-xuge.title: Battle of Xuge
sa-707-xuge.summary: Conflict between the Zhou king and the State of Zheng, illustrating limited royal control over strong states.

sa-651-kuiqiu.title: Kuiqiu conference
sa-651-kuiqiu.summary: A major interstate conference led by Duke Huan of Qi, often cited as a key moment in hegemon politics.

sa-597-bi.title: Battle of Bi
sa-597-bi.summary: A pivotal battle in the Jin–Chu rivalry that influenced the balance among major powers.

sa-594-tax.title: “First tax on fields” (trad.)
sa-594-tax.summary: A traditionally recorded shift in taxation, often discussed as part of Spring and Autumn institutional change.
